figures.caption: Table 1: Three representative contamination mechanisms. Each row follows the event structure: trigger x, retrieved context r, and a resulting violation. The judge taxonomy includes additional subtypes (Appendix C).
Table 7: Categorization of all 159 judge–human disagreements.

Figure 2: Temporal memory contamination: (a) event decomposition, (b) trigger-probe protocol applied across five deployment streams, and (c) the resulting metric qa(ℓ) across seven memory architectures.

Figure 4: Eight memory architectures positioned along six implementation-derived dimensions. Coordinates are computed from implementation properties alone, independent of safety outcomes. See Appendix F.

Figure 3: Memory-induced violation rate qa(ℓ) across three datasets under sequential prefixes (a–c), blockshuffled prefixes with B=50 (d–f), and full shuffle with B=1 (g–i). Shaded bands show variability across probe triggers.
Figure 5: Retrieval-time violation prediction across Medical and University Registrar. Mean precision, recall, and F1 averaged across all eight memory architectures.
Figure 6: Memory-induced violation rate on Claw-like agents across four LLM backends and two platforms. Solid lines: OpenClaw. Dashed lines: SecLaw.
